title: Mini Bookshelf with Front Trim Legs
summary: A small 1x12 bookshelf with 1x2 legs on all four corners and a two-piece top that closes a small reveal gap at the front — a compact scale distinct from this catalog's taller face-frame bookshelves.
description:
This is a genuinely small bookshelf — under 30" tall — built as a plain 1x12 box first, then dressed with applied 1x2 legs at all four corners rather than a full face frame. Mark the shelf joint locations on both the inside and outside of the side panels before fastening anything, since the boards are 3/4" thick and it's easy to fasten a shelf a half-inch off from where the outside marks say it should land if you're only checking one face.

**Support cleats under the bottom shelf carry real weight** — attached to both the sides and the underside of the bottom shelf, they're what keeps a small shelf like this from sagging at the corners under books. The four legs attach next, sitting flush with both the top edge of the sides and the outside edge of the sides — a small but specific alignment worth checking with a square before the glue sets.

Shelf trim covers the front edges of each shelf, fastened first to the shelf itself, then anchored again through the legs into the trim's ends for a genuinely rigid joint. **The top is built in two pieces, front and back, with the gap between them minimized** rather than built as one solid board — worth dry-fitting before final attachment to make sure that seam stays tight.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 12 ft 1x12, cut two sides 28 1/4" long, two shelves 20 1/2", and the top back piece 24"; all are 11 1/4" wide, which fixes the shelf depth. From the three 8 ft 1x2s, cut four legs 30 1/4", four shelf trims 19", two bottom shelf supports 11 1/2", and the top front piece 24". Those numbers interlock: the 20 1/2" shelves fitting between the two 3/4" sides make the case 22" wide, which is why the back panel is 22" and why the 19" shelf trim fits between the two 1 1/2" front legs. Cut the 1/4" back panel 22" x 29" last, checked against the case you actually built.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: Mark each shelf's position on both the inside and the outside face of each side panel. The boards are 3/4" thick, so a shelf can end up half an inch off the outside mark if you lay it out on one face and then drive screws from the other - marking both faces makes that mistake impossible. The bottom shelf sits at the bottom of the case and the middle shelf goes wherever you want it, so dry-fit before committing. Fasten through the sides into the shelf ends with glue and 2" screws, two or three per joint, then check the case's diagonals once both shelves are in.

steps.title: Add the support cleats
steps.body: Fit an 11 1/2" 1x2 cleat under each end of the bottom shelf, screwed both into the side panel and up into the underside of the shelf with 2" screws and glue. These are not trim: a shelf that is only end-screwed will sag at the corners under a row of books, and these cleats are what carry that load out into the sides. Keep their top edges tight against the shelf's underside so there is no gap for the shelf to settle into.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Stand a 30 1/4" leg at each of the four corners, flush with the top edge of the side panel and flush with the side panel's outside face, so the leg's 1 1/2" width wraps the corner cleanly. Check that alignment with the square before nailing - it is a small, specific fit, and it is the difference between crisp corners and a shelf that looks approximate. Attach with 2" nails and glue into the sides and into the shelf ends behind them. The legs run past the bottom of the case, which is what lifts the shelf clear of the floor.

steps.title: Attach the shelf trim
steps.body: Fit a 19" shelf trim across the front edge of each shelf, between the two front legs, with its top edge flush with the shelf's top surface so the trim reads as a thicker shelf. Nail through its face into the shelf's front edge with 1-1/4" nails and glue, then come back and drive 2" nails through the outside of each front leg into the trim's end grain; that second set is what makes the joint rigid rather than merely closed. The cut list gives four trims for two shelves, so lay all four against the case front and settle where the second pair goes before nailing anything.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: Set the 24" x 11 1/4" top back piece across the top of the case, overhanging 1" at each end of the 22" case, and nail it into the top edges of the sides and legs with 2" nails and glue. Then fit the 24" 1x2 top front piece along the front edge, pushing the seam between the two as tight as it will close: the top is deliberately made in two pieces rather than one board, so that seam is the only thing that can give it away. Dry-fit both pieces and slide them together before any glue goes on, and clamp across the seam while it sets.

steps.title: Attach the back and finish
steps.body: Square the case one last time, then glue and nail the 22" x 29" back panel on with 1-1/4" nails right around the perimeter and into the back edge of each shelf; nailing into the shelves as well as the sides is what stops a small case racking under load. Fill every nail hole with wood filler, let it dry, and sand the whole piece, easing the leg corners and the shelf trim edges where hands will land. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Small bookshelf with applied corner leg trim
